citation: Baker HP, Kahn AJ, Rosenberg S, Qin C, Portney D, Athiviraham A. NFL total quarter back rating (QBR) is not decreased following sport related concussion injury. Sport Sciences for Health. 2021. doi:10.1007/s11332-020-00705-8
abstract: This retrospective case-control study tested whether NFL quarterback performance, measured by total quarterback rating (QBR), declined after return to play from sport-related concussion. The authors identified 42 regular-season quarterback concussion cases from 2006 to 2019 using public NFL injury reports and matched them 1:1 by age to quarterbacks with other musculoskeletal injuries. Quarterbacks with concussion missed 2.83 weeks on average versus 2.33 weeks in controls. The concussion group had lower career QBR than controls, but the key return-to-play comparison showed no statistically significant difference between season QBR and weekly QBR in the first 6 weeks after return when concussion cases were compared with controls. The authors concluded that QBR did not decrease in the weeks after quarterbacks returned from concussion.
tldr: In NFL quarterbacks who returned from reported concussions, QBR did not fall below season expectation more than it did in age-matched injured controls, but QBR is a blunt public-facing performance metric and should not be treated as proof of neurologic recovery.
methods: Retrospective Level III case-control study of NFL quarterbacks listed with concussion from 2006-2019; preseason injuries and players who did not return during the regular season were excluded; public injury reports and transaction sources were cross-checked; 42 concussion cases were age-matched to 42 quarterbacks with non-concussion injuries using MEDCALC; outcomes included age, years in NFL, weeks missed, career QBR, season QBR, and weekly QBR for the first 6 weeks after return; independent samples t tests were run in SPSS with p<0.05.
findings: The concussion and control groups did not differ significantly in age, NFL experience, or weeks missed. Quarterbacks with concussion averaged 27.2 years old, 5.9 NFL seasons, and 2.8 missed weeks. Career QBR was lower in the concussion group than controls (81.2 vs 89.1; p=0.01), but weekly QBR changes versus season QBR were not significantly different between groups at weeks 1-6 after return (p values 0.79, 0.11, 0.87, 0.28, 0.18, and 0.51). The study did not support the hypothesis that post-concussion quarterback performance is detectably worse after clearance.
limitations: Public injury reporting may miss or underclassify concussions; concussion severity, symptom course, neurocognitive testing, and physiologic recovery were unavailable; QBR may not be sensitive to subtle decision-making, vision, processing speed, or risk tolerance changes; small quarterback sample limits power; concomitant injuries were not modeled; a quarterback with an apparent severe concussion and no return-season QBR was excluded.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the main operational message is not that concussion is harmless to performance, but that box-score outcomes can look normal after RTP. Staff should keep concussion management anchored to symptom evaluation, graded exertion, vestibular/ocular findings, cognitive load, sleep, and medical clearance rather than relying on practice or game productivity as evidence of full recovery.
athleteDev: Quarterbacks need protected development of decision-making under fatigue and contact exposure, but concussion education and honest reporting remain central. Building a culture where QBs can report symptoms without fear of losing status is more actionable than trying to infer brain recovery from passing efficiency after return.
rtp: The paper addresses post-concussion RTP outcomes but does not validate RTP criteria. It supports a cautious interpretation: once NFL quarterbacks were medically cleared and returned, QBR was not detectably reduced, yet RTP should remain individualized and criteria-based because performance metrics cannot rule out persisting symptoms, physiologic vulnerability, or reinjury risk.

citation: Burgess CJ, Singh V, Lygrisse KA, Choy K, Cohn RM, Bitterman A. National Football League wide receivers and running backs have decreased production following ACL reconstruction: An evaluation of fantasy football performance as an outcome measure. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation. 2021. doi:10.1016/j.asmr.2021.09.021
abstract: This retrospective study used fantasy football scoring and position-specific statistics to evaluate NFL offensive skill player production before and after isolated unilateral ACL reconstruction from 1988 to 2017. The final sample included 13 quarterbacks, 30 running backs, and 29 wide receivers who played at least three regular-season games before and after injury; tight ends were excluded because of small numbers. Matched controls with similar demographics were used for post-index comparisons. Quarterbacks showed no statistically significant decline in fantasy points or performance after ACL reconstruction. Running backs had markedly lower career fantasy points after ACL reconstruction than controls (129.6 vs 553.6; p<0.0001) and lower points per game (4.4 vs 11.2; p<0.0001). Wide receivers also had lower career fantasy points (145.3 vs 460.9; p=0.002) and lower points per game (5.0 vs 7.7; p=0.042).
tldr: ACL reconstruction did not meaningfully reduce NFL quarterback production, but running backs and wide receivers had large post-surgery drops in fantasy output, games played, and position-specific production compared with controls.
methods: Retrospective review of public NFL injury and performance data for QBs, RBs, WRs, and TEs with isolated unilateral ACL injury from 1988-2017; included players had at least three regular-season games before and after injury; prior ACL injury, concomitant ipsilateral knee ligament/meniscus injury, and other concurrent injuries were excluded; TEs were excluded for low sample size; matched controls were selected; position statistics and fantasy points were compared before versus after ACL reconstruction and against post-index controls using independent two-sided t tests in SPSS.
findings: Thirteen QBs, 30 RBs, and 29 WRs met criteria. QBs did not differ significantly from controls in post-ACL fantasy points or most performance variables. RBs showed significant post-ACL declines in games played, rushing volume, rushing yards, receiving production, touchdowns, points per game, points per season, and total fantasy points. WRs showed significant declines in games played, targets, receptions, receiving yards, touchdowns, and fantasy scoring compared with controls. The authors emphasized that combine-style physical measures may return, but game production for RBs and WRs may remain lower.
limitations: Retrospective public-data design; small position-specific samples; fantasy football points are not a validated medical outcome; talent, role, depth chart opportunity, team context, coaching, and contract status are difficult to control; surgical technique, graft choice, rehab quality, and complications were unavailable; players naturally had fewer career years remaining after injury, which may partly explain lower cumulative output.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, ACLR expectations should be position-specific. RBs and WRs depend heavily on cutting, deceleration, route separation, contact balance, and explosive reacceleration, so medical clearance and strong testing numbers may not equal restored football production. Return plans should include progressive open-field speed, route/cut density, reactive change of direction, contact tolerance, and workload restoration before assuming full role readiness.
athleteDev: Skill players should develop durable knee capacity before injury: eccentric quad and hamstring strength, hip/trunk control, deceleration mechanics, single-leg stiffness, landing quality, and repeated sprint/cut exposure. Development plans for RBs and WRs after ACLR should also target confidence, proprioception, and repeatability of high-speed football actions, not only peak strength.
rtp: Highly relevant to RTP. The study suggests QBs may return to statistical production more reliably than RBs and WRs, while RB/WR clearance should be framed as return to performance over months, not just return to participation. RTP criteria should combine strength symmetry, hop/change-of-direction testing, GPS workload, position-specific drills, contact progression, psychological readiness, and staged snap-count management.

citation: NFL-PFATS. Sports Science Research Update: April 2023. 2023.
abstract: The April 2023 NFL-PFATS Sports Science Research Update is a 23-page practitioner digest summarizing recent papers on subjective monitoring, hamstring mechanics, hamstring injury classification and RTP, eccentric prevention programs, compression garments, recovery protocols, nutrition/ergogenic aids, hydration bioavailability, nonoperative NFL hip/core injuries, and training-load metrics. A major theme is hamstring injury management: the update highlights high-speed running EMG differences across hamstring muscles, the London hamstring consensus series on classification, operative management, rehabilitation/running/RTP, evidence that reduced match exposure before injury may signal hamstring risk, and medical-officer views that communication, high-speed exposure, and load management are key modifiable risk factors. It also includes an NFL-specific study showing all players returned after nonoperative hip/core injury, but defensive players had fewer games and lower performance ratings after hip/core injuries than controls.
tldr: April 2023 is a hamstring-heavy digest: classify injuries better, do not use imaging alone to clear RTP, build high-speed exposure, coordinate medical and coaching decisions, and monitor recovery with more than one metric.
methods: Monthly evidence digest compiled from recent peer-reviewed sport science and sports medicine papers. The PDF provides article titles, citations, abstracts or structured summaries, and key-point excerpts rather than a new original study. Topics were synthesized here for football sport science application, with emphasis on papers most transferable to American football: hamstring injury prevention/RTP, high-speed running, recovery, hip/core injury, athlete monitoring, and training-load measurement.
findings: Key applied findings include: world-class athletes' honesty in subjective monitoring is shaped by trust, perceived compassion, and how staff use the data; hamstring activation during high-speed running is heterogeneous across biceps femoris, semitendinosus, and semimembranosus; eccentric hamstring programs can reduce lower-extremity injury risk, but implementation details matter; the London consensus recommends MRI for diagnosis/prognosis but not as the sole rehab or RTS decision tool; hamstring rehabilitation should integrate running progression and sport demands; reduced recent match exposure was associated with hamstring injury in professional soccer; European club CMOs rated poor medical-coaching communication and lack of regular high-speed exposure as important hamstring risk factors; nonoperative NFL hip/core injuries allowed RTP, but defensive players showed reduced post-injury games and performance.
limitations: This is a curated digest, not a systematic review or primary dataset. Included studies vary widely in design, sport, population, and quality. Many papers are soccer-based and require translation to American football demands. The update provides brief summaries rather than full methods, effect sizes, and risk-of-bias appraisal for every article.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, this update supports a shared hamstring operating model between sports medicine, S&C, sport science, and position coaches. The practical stack is: regular high-speed exposure, objective strength and sprint progression, clear communication on practice content, subjective monitoring delivered with trust, and caution against using MRI resolution alone as a green light. Hip/core injuries in defensive players deserve close workload and performance follow-up after return.
athleteDev: Development programming should expose athletes year-round to sprinting, acceleration, deceleration, trunk/hip control, and eccentric hamstring strength in doses that fit the weekly plan. Monitoring should include both athlete-reported readiness and objective field/weight-room data, with staff behavior encouraging truthful reporting instead of compliance theater.
rtp: The April update is strongly RTP relevant. The London hamstring consensus emphasizes classification, criteria-based rehabilitation, running progression, and sport-specific return rather than imaging-only decisions. The hip/core NFL summary also reinforces that RTP can occur while performance remains affected, so return-to-performance tracking should continue after medical clearance.

citation: NFL-PFATS. Sports Science Research Update: August 2023. 2023.
abstract: The August 2023 NFL-PFATS Sports Science Research Update is a 21-page digest covering football brain health, tackling technique, sodium bicarbonate, CBD, commotio cordis, the Amsterdam 2022 concussion consensus, hamstring fatigue and fiber typology, creatine and brain health, neurodiversity, plant-based diets, high-speed sprint exposure programming, psychological readiness for RTP, and sleep-wake disorders after concussion. Football-specific highlights include a 1000-tackle NCAA Division I video study showing head-up, at-or-above-waist, inside-shoulder tackles had the highest success rates; the Amsterdam concussion statement with updated CRT6, SCAT6, and SCOAT6 tools and support for early aerobic exercise and cervicovestibular rehab; a review emphasizing AED access and rapid response for commotio cordis; and a worldwide practitioner survey showing broad agreement that high-speed and sprint exposure matter but inconsistent definitions and monitoring methods.
tldr: August 2023 centers on football safety and return-to-performance: teach safer and more effective tackling, update concussion workflows to Amsterdam tools, treat sleep and psychological readiness as RTP variables, and standardize high-speed running exposure.
methods: Monthly practitioner digest of recently published sport science and sports medicine literature. The PDF summarizes selected peer-reviewed studies, consensus statements, reviews, and editorials with abstracts and key points. This record synthesizes the digest for American football application, prioritizing tackling, concussion, emergency action planning, high-speed exposure, hamstring fatigue, nutrition/supplements, psychological readiness, and sleep.
findings: Head-up tackles were successful in 76.2% of attempts versus 55.1% for head-down tackles, and inside-shoulder tackles had the highest success rate at 93.2% in the NCAA video study. Oral sodium bicarbonate improved repeated sprint time and Yo-Yo IR2 performance in recreational team-sport athletes, while topical bicarbonate improved repeated sprint time but not blood acid-base measures. The Amsterdam concussion consensus introduced updated recognition, acute assessment, and office assessment tools, discouraged using computerized neurocognitive testing alone, and supported early physical activity plus cervicovestibular rehab when indicated. Sleep-wake disturbances after SRC were described as independent risk factors for prolonged recovery. Practitioners agreed high-speed and sprint exposure is important, but definitions, thresholds, and scheduling vary widely.
limitations: The digest is not a formal systematic review and does not rate evidence quality across all included papers. Several studies are non-football, small-sample, review-based, or focused on soccer/team-sport settings rather than NCAA football. Supplement findings may not generalize to elite collision-sport athletes, and some topics such as CBD and creatine-brain health remain emerging.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the strongest takeaways are operational: reinforce head-up/inside-shoulder tackling as both safer and more effective; keep AED access, rehearsal, and commotio cordis response visible in emergency action planning; update concussion processes around CRT6/SCAT6/SCOAT6 concepts; screen sleep after concussion; and align staff definitions for high-speed and sprint exposure so weekly plans are consistent across position groups.
athleteDev: Development should connect skill safety with performance. Tackling technique work should reward head-up leverage and inside-shoulder mechanics, not only collision outcome. Sprint exposure should be planned for starters and developmental players, with nonstarters receiving enough high-speed work to maintain readiness. Nutrition and supplement conversations should be evidence-led and compliance-aware.
rtp: RTP themes are prominent: concussion management should include updated assessment tools, early controlled activity, targeted cervicovestibular rehab, sleep management, and monitoring for symptoms beyond 4 weeks. Orthopedic RTP should include psychological readiness and fear-of-reinjury screening, while high-speed exposure programming helps bridge rehab completion to game-speed availability.

citation: NFL-PFATS. Sports Science Research Update: February 2023. 2023.
abstract: The February 2023 NFL-PFATS Sports Science Research Update is a 26-page digest summarizing research on ankle ligament repair, CBD after exercise, air quality and NFL/MLB errors, acceleration/deceleration event definitions, cold-water immersion, NFL crowd effects on penalties, hamstring injury trends, elevated NFL injury rates after the COVID-19 disruption, head acceleration measurement standards, hamstring architecture after injury, microcycle rest planning, post-game hamstring strength recovery, football age of first exposure, CMJ metric selection, supplement risk, sleep after concussion, concussion knowledge in British American football, heat and decision-making, fixture congestion, and repeated high-intensity efforts. Football-relevant highlights include an NFL injury-report study showing 2021 injury incidence remained elevated versus 2018-2019 and 2020, a UEFA hamstring study showing hamstrings rose to 24% of all injuries with many recurrences within 2 months of return, and CHAMP guidance on better head acceleration measurement practices.
tldr: February 2023 is a broad load-management and injury-risk digest: hamstring burden is rising, NFL injuries stayed elevated after the COVID-era disruption, recovery/rest timing matters, and metric definitions can change what staff think they are measuring.
methods: Monthly evidence digest summarizing recent peer-reviewed articles and reviews. The PDF provides citations, abstracts, structured summaries, and practical points rather than original data collection. This record synthesizes the update for Baylor football staff, emphasizing American football injury surveillance, hamstring recurrence, recovery modalities, microcycle planning, monitoring metrics, concussion/sleep, environmental stress, and supplement risk.
findings: Early lateral ankle ligament repair in 147 elite athletes produced 100% return to preinjury sport level with median RTS of 69 days, while associated deltoid, syndesmosis, or osteochondral injuries delayed return. CBD did not reduce inflammation, fatigue, or performance loss after eccentric muscle damage in trained female athletes. Air quality predicted MLB errors and NFL quarterback mistakes in one environmental study. Changing acceleration/deceleration minimum-duration criteria changed which player actions were detected. Cold-water immersion outperformed several common recovery methods for soreness but was not clearly superior for all performance outcomes. UEFA hamstring injuries doubled as a share of injuries over 21 seasons, reaching 24% of all injuries and 20% of absence days in 2021/22, with about 18% recurrences and many within 2 months. NFL 2021 injury incidence was higher than pre-COVID seasons and increased after week 4. AFE to football had weak associations with self-reported outcomes and was not associated with medical diagnoses in former NFL players.
limitations: This is a curated monthly update, not a systematic review. Studies span different sports, levels, countries, and methods. Several findings come from soccer or AFL and need careful American football translation. Public NFL injury-report studies undercount practice and less severe injuries. The digest format limits full appraisal of bias, confounding, and measurement reliability.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the update argues for precision in monitoring and recovery decisions. Staff should standardize acceleration/deceleration definitions, interpret GPS events by actual football actions, maintain hamstring prevention through the first 2 months after RTP, plan off-feet recovery strategically after games, and avoid overpromising CBD or any supplement without evidence and compliance review. Mid-to-late season injury surveillance deserves special attention.
athleteDev: Athlete development should build resilience to repeated high-intensity efforts, sprinting, deceleration, heat, and congested weeks. CMJ and GPS metrics should be selected for a purpose: performance profiling, fatigue monitoring, or rehab testing. Hamstring history should trigger individualized post-game strength monitoring and sustained eccentric/high-speed exposure rather than a short rehab-only emphasis.
rtp: RTP relevance is high across ankle, hamstring, concussion, and monitoring topics. The digest supports criteria-based ankle RTS with attention to associated injuries, extended hamstring surveillance for at least 2 months after return, sleep screening after sport-related concussion, and using CMJ/GPS metrics that match the specific RTP question rather than collecting default numbers.
